refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total operational time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement for remote equipment control. Refinement clarifies that 'link availability' means sustained bidirectional connectivity (commands to vehicle, telemetry from vehicle) and establishes the quantitative target (0.999) as the acceptance criterion. High safety criticality demands explicit measurement methodology and evaluation window definition.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) logged at both Teleoperation and Vehicle endpoints; post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime ratio; cross-validation against network infrastructure logs and safety event records.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition (e.g., vehicle halt) upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths to achieve 0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics for post-evaluation audit.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path network routing (primary + backup) with automatic failover at the network layer; monitor link health via periodic heartbeat messages; log state transitions.
DP-B: Implement redundant communication modules (LTE + satellite) with application-level link selection logic; operator-initiated switchover with manual confirmation.
DP-C: Implement single primary network path with passive monitoring; rely on underlying carrier SLA for availability; no active failover.
DP-D: Implement triple-redundant network paths (LTE + satellite + mesh) with intelligent load balancing and automatic failover; continuous health monitoring and predictive link quality assessment.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement dual-path network routing (primary + backup) with automatic failover at the network layer; monitor link health via periodic heartbeat messages; log state transitions.

potential_sources: DP-A couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure design (must specify failover latency, heartbeat interval, and backup path capacity).
DP-A couples to Safety Services via safe-state transition logic (vehicle halt on link loss must be coordinated with safety interlocks).
DP-A couples to Vehicle telemetry reporting (heartbeat and link state must be included in telemetry stream).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert). DP-A avoids over-specification (DP-D) and under-specification (DP-C) for high-safety-criticality context. Automatic failover (DP-A) is preferred over manual (DP-B) to reduce operator reaction time and human error risk.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Backup network path (e.g., satellite or secondary LTE carrier) is contractually available and meets latency/bandwidth requirements.
Assumption: Heartbeat interval and failover detection latency are compatible with vehicle safe-state transition time (e.g., emergency stop must complete before operator loses situational awareness).
Risk: If backup path capacity is insufficient, failover may degrade command responsiveness, potentially violating safety requirements.
Risk: Heartbeat false-positives (link declared lost when still operational) may trigger unnecessary safe-state transitions, reducing productivity.
Risk: Network provider SLA may not guarantee 0.999 availability; contractual alignment required.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) heartbeat interval and timeout threshold, (2) failover latency budget, (3) backup path capacity and latency characteristics, (4) safe-state transition sequence and timing. Coordinate with Safety Services DP to ensure link-loss detection triggers are synchronized with vehicle halt logic.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Teleoperation module owns the dual-path network routing logic, link health monitoring (heartbeat generation/reception), failover decision logic, and link state logging. The module interfaces with Vehicle via Telemetry (bidirectional) and SafetyAlert (outbound), and with Safety Services via SafetyAlert (outbound) for safe-state coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional command-and-telemetry stream. Teleoperation sends Tasking commands to Vehicle; Vehicle returns Telemetry (including heartbeat/link-state indicators). Failover logic monitors Telemetry reception; link loss triggers SafetyAlert.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound operator commands to Vehicle. Tasking is suspended or redirected to safe-state commands upon link loss detection.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety notifications to Safety Services. Link loss event triggers SafetyAlert to initiate vehicle halt and operator notification.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network infrastructure (primary + backup paths). Teleoperation module abstracts dual-path routing and failover; Network Link provides physical/logical connectivity.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Heartbeat generation, failover decision, and link state logging are core teleoperation functions. Minor diffusion: network infrastructure (NGHE Solution or external carrier) owns physical backup path provisioning; Vehicle owns heartbeat reception and safe-state transition.

Safety Services could own link-loss detection and safe-state triggering, but this couples safety logic to network monitoring. Better separation: Teleoperation detects link loss and raises SafetyAlert; Safety Services responds by enforcing safe state. Containment score reflects secondary responsibility for safe-state coordination.

Vehicle could implement heartbeat reception and link-loss response, but link availability is a system-level concern (operator-centric). Vehicle owns safe-state transition execution, not link availability measurement. Low containment score reflects that Vehicle is a consumer of link availability, not the owner.

NGHE Solution (system-level) could own overall link availability architecture, but this is too coarse. Teleoperation is the appropriate subsystem owner. NGHE Solution owns integration and acceptance testing of link availability across all modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module contains link availability logic (dual-path routing, heartbeat monitoring, failover decision, state logging). External dependencies: Vehicle (heartbeat reception, safe-state execution), Safety Services (safe-state coordination), Network Link (physical backup path). Interfaces: Telemetry (inout), Tasking (out), SafetyAlert (out), Network Link (inout).
containment_risks: Risk: If heartbeat logic is duplicated across Teleoperation and Vehicle, link-state inconsistency may occur (e.g., Teleoperation declares link lost, but Vehicle still receives commands). Mitigation: Single source of truth for link state in Teleoperation; Vehicle responds to SafetyAlert from Teleoperation.

Risk: If backup network path is managed by external carrier (not NGHE Solution), failover latency and availability are outside Teleoperation control. Mitigation: Contractual SLA with carrier; Teleoperation monitors backup path health independently.

Risk: If safe-state transition logic is split between Teleoperation (link-loss detection) and Safety Services (halt execution), timing misalignment may occur. Mitigation: Explicit SafetyAlert interface with defined latency budget; Safety Services must respond within [X] ms.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify ownership boundaries: (1) Teleoperation owns link availability measurement and failover logic. (2) Safety Services owns safe-state enforcement and operator notification. (3) Vehicle owns heartbeat reception and safe-state execution. (4) NGHE Solution owns network infrastructure provisioning and acceptance testing. Define explicit interface contracts: Telemetry heartbeat format, SafetyAlert latency budget, Network Link failover latency.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (dual-path routing with automatic failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥ 0.999). DP-A is sufficient to achieve the quantitative target if backup path is provisioned and failover latency is within safe-state transition budget. No logical gap between requirement and solution concept.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Heartbeat logic may be partially implemented in Vehicle (reception) and Teleoperation (generation/monitoring). Mitigation: Define heartbeat protocol in Telemetry interface contract; Vehicle is passive receiver, Teleoperation is active monitor.

Diffusion Risk 2: Safe-state transition is split between Teleoperation (link-loss detection) and Safety Services (halt execution). Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface defines trigger and response latency; clear ownership boundary.

Diffusion Risk 3: Network infrastructure (backup path provisioning) is external to NGHE Solution (carrier-managed). Mitigation: Contractual SLA; Teleoperation monitors backup path health via periodic probes.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the appropriate IM boundary for link availability logic. Containment score 0.95 indicates minimal diffusion. External dependencies (Vehicle, Safety Services, Network Link) are well-defined via interfaces. No evidence of scattered or duplicated logic across modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Link availability is measurable via continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) at Teleoperation endpoint. Post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime ratio is straightforward. Acceptance criterion (0.999) is quantitative and testable. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Telemetry interface to log link state, (2) post-evaluation audit of logs, (3) cross-validation with network infrastructure logs.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation IM with clear sub-allocations: FR-NGHE-010.1 (link-loss detection) → Teleoperation, FR-NGHE-010.2 (safe-state transition) → Safety Services + Vehicle, FR-NGHE-010.3 (network redundancy) → NGHE Solution + Network Link, FR-NGHE-010.4 (metrics logging) → Teleoperation. Allocation is traceable and verifiable.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine DP-A to specify heartbeat interval (e.g., 100 ms), failover detection latency (e.g., 500 ms), and safe-state transition time budget. Ensure failover latency + safe-state transition time ≤ operator reaction time (e.g., 2 seconds).

Revision 2: Define Telemetry interface contract to include heartbeat/link-state fields, format, and timing. Define SafetyAlert interface contract to include link-loss trigger, response latency budget, and safe-state command sequence.
Revision 3: Establish contractual SLA with network carrier for backup path availability, latency, and bandwidth. Define monitoring protocol for Teleoperation to verify backup path health.

Revision 4: Allocate sub-FR-NGHE-010.2 (safe-state transition) to Safety Services and Vehicle with explicit timing requirements. Ensure safe-state transition is atomic and cannot be interrupted by operator commands.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs (link-loss detection, safe-state transition, network redundancy, metrics logging). DP-A is sound and well-coupled to Teleoperation IM, but sub-FRs must be refined and allocated to specific modules (Teleoperation, Safety Services, Vehicle, NGHE Solution) before detailed design. High safety criticality demands rigorous decomposition and interface specification at the next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss (absence of heartbeat) within 500 milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: Upon link loss detection, the teleoperation system shall issue a SafetyAlert to Safety Services within 100 milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The vehicle shall transition to safe state (halt) within 1 second of receiving SafetyAlert from Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall maintain dual-path network routing (primary + backup) with automatic failover latency ≤ 200 milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The network infrastructure shall provide backup path (e.g., satellite or secondary LTE) with availability ≥ 0.995 and latency ≤ 500 milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.6: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions (up/down) with timestamp and path identifier for post-evaluation audit.

revise.why: DP-A requires refinement to specify heartbeat interval, failover latency budget, and safe-state transition timing. Current DP is conceptually sound but lacks implementation detail needed for sub-FR decomposition. Recommend revising DP-A to include: (1) heartbeat protocol (interval, timeout, format), (2) failover decision logic (threshold, latency), (3) safe-state command sequence, (4) logging format and retention policy.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Backup network path (e.g., satellite or secondary LTE carrier) is contractually available and meets latency/bandwidth requirements.

Assumption 2: Heartbeat interval (e.g., 100 ms) and failover detection latency (e.g., 500 ms) are compatible with vehicle safe-state transition time (e.g., 1 second) and operator reaction time (e.g., 2 seconds).
Assumption 3: Network provider SLA guarantees backup path availability ≥ 0.995 and latency ≤ 500 ms.
Assumption 4: Teleoperation module has sufficient computational capacity to monitor dual-path routing and execute failover logic without impacting command/telemetry latency.
Assumption 5: Vehicle can execute safe-state transition (halt) within 1 second of receiving SafetyAlert, regardless of current operational state.
open_risks: Risk 1: Backup network path capacity may be insufficient to support full teleoperation bandwidth during failover, degrading command responsiveness and potentially violating safety requirements.
Risk 2: Heartbeat false-positives (link declared lost when still operational) may trigger unnecessary safe-state transitions, reducing productivity and operator confidence.
Risk 3: Failover latency may exceed safe-state transition time budget, resulting in operator commands being lost during transition.
Risk 4: Network provider SLA may not guarantee 0.999 availability; contractual alignment required before acceptance testing.
Risk 5: Teleoperation module may not have sufficient visibility into backup path health; external monitoring (e.g., carrier-provided metrics) may be required.
Risk 6: Safe-state transition logic may be interrupted by operator commands if Tasking interface is not properly gated during link loss.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Network carrier SLA contract specifying backup path availability, latency, and bandwidth.
Evidence 2: Telemetry interface specification including heartbeat format, interval, and timeout threshold.
Evidence 3: SafetyAlert interface specification including link-loss trigger, response latency budget, and safe-state command sequence.
Evidence 4: Vehicle safe-state transition specification including halt time, power-down sequence, and operator notification.
Evidence 5: Teleoperation module design document specifying heartbeat generation/reception logic, failover decision algorithm, and logging format.
Evidence 6: Network infrastructure design document specifying dual-path routing, failover mechanism, and backup path provisioning.
Evidence 7: Acceptance test plan specifying link availability measurement methodology, evaluation period, and acceptance criteria.
Evidence 8: Safety analysis (FMEA/FTA) identifying link-loss failure modes and mitigation strategies.
